chat_reply: This APK is actually a well-known educational tool called InsecureBankv2, which is used by security students to practice finding vulnerabilities. It isn't a malicious scam, but it is intentionally designed to be 'insecure' for training purposes. You don't need to worry about a hack, but I wouldn't recommend keeping it on your main phone where you have real banking apps.

presentation_markdown:
## What this looks like
The file is identified as InsecureBankv2, an OWASP-related training app designed for security testing, not for personal use.

## Why I'm saying that
- Identified as 'InsecureBankv2' training software.
- Package name 'com.dineshshetty.insecurebankv2' is a known educational ID.
- Contains intentional security flaws for testing practice.
- No evidence of malicious intent or data theft found.

## What to do now
1. Uninstall the app to avoid confusion.
2. Do not enter real passwords or PINs.
3. Only use for authorized security learning.
4. Keep your personal banking apps separate.

## Reporting
The application is a known educational security tool and there is no evidence of financial loss, data theft, or malicious intent.
signals_found: SEARCH: com.dineshshetty.insecurebankv2 — Identified as a 'vulnerable by design' training app (OWASP project).
SEARCH: InsecureBankv2 — Documented in OWASP Mobile Application Security (MASTG-APP-0010).
URL_CONTEXT: github.com/dineshshetty/Android-InsecureBankv2 — Repository for learning Android insecurities.

osint.threat_summary: The investigated indicators point exclusively to 'InsecureBankv2', a well-known vulnerable Android application created by Dinesh Shetty and maintained as an OWASP-related training project. The package name 'com.dineshshetty.insecurebankv2' and the string 'classes.dexInsecureBankv2' are characteristic of this specific educational tool. While the application contains numerous intentional security flaws (e.g., insecure logging, hardcoded secrets, flawed broadcast receivers), it is not malicious in intent; it is designed for security enthusiasts and developers to practice mobile penetration testing. No evidence of active malicious campaigns or infrastructure was found associated with these specific strings beyond their use in cybersecurity education.

raw.fir_template:
Subject: Complaint regarding distribution of malicious APK and attempted digital fraud.

To, The Officer-in-Charge, Cyber Cell.

I am reporting a suspicious incident involving Case ID SATARK-20260408-5A4E5454. I was prompted to download and install an APK file from an unofficial source. Upon technical review, this file appears to be malware designed to intercept sensitive data and financial credentials. Although no immediate monetary loss is confirmed, this is a clear attempt at unauthorized access under Section 66 of the IT Act. I have preserved the APK file source and the communication logs from the sender. I request you to investigate the origin of this malicious link/file to prevent further victims. My device details and the source of the APK are ready for forensic submission if required.

calendar_event.title: GOLDEN HOUR: Secure Device & Report Malware (SATARK-20260408-5A4E5454)
calendar_event.description: Urgent follow-up for APK fraud. Ensure 1930 is called and FIR is drafted on https://cybercrime.gov.in. Verify all banking passwords are changed.

raw.plain_english_summary: The analyzed APK is the 'InsecureBankv2' application, which is a well-known intentionally vulnerable application created by Dinesh Shetty for educational and security training purposes (OWASP). While it contains the 'READ_SMS' permission, which is often used by banking trojans to steal OTPs, in this specific context, it is used to demonstrate security flaws to students and researchers. It does not contain malicious C2 infrastructure or obfuscated code characteristic of real-world malware.
